Title: Biosynthesis and biotechnological production of ginsenosides

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Panax ginseng; Arabidopsis thaliana; Platycodon grandiflorus
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate in the early MVA pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High in petioles of 2-week-old seedlings; abundant in roots of 3-year-old and 6-year-old ginseng; active in root vasculature
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous dark exposure promotes expression; mevinolin inhibition reduces ginsenosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes triterpene and ginsenoside accumulation; overexpression increases total ginsenosides about 1.5- to 2-fold in transgenic ginseng adventitious roots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in P. ginseng, Arabidopsis, and Platycodon; inhibitor assay with mevinolin; promoter activity analysis; complementation of Arabidopsis hmgr mutant
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgHMGR1 is a key upstream control gene for ginsenoside biosynthesis in P. ginseng. 2. Its expression is root- and vasculature-associated and induced by darkness. 3. Overexpression increases triterpenes/sterols and total ginsenosides, whereas inhibition lowers ginsenoside content.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Mainly root epidermis/periderm and outer cortex; biosynthetic transcripts enriched in vasculature/resin ducts/phloem
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression also increases phytosterols and other triterpenes, indicating carbon flux is not exclusive to ginsenosides.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review summarizes this as one of the best-supported engineering targets; PgHMGR2 failed to complement AtHMGR1 mutant unlike PgHMGR1.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for metabolic engineering to raise total ginsenosides; useful for stacking with downstream pathway genes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces HMG-CoA to mevalonate in the MVA pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: HMGR family is rate-limiting; specific decisive role of PgHMGR2 less established than PgHMGR1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Weak in petioles of 2-week-old seedlings; expression gradually increases with plant development; active in root vasculature
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely associated with age-dependent ginsenoside accumulation in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sequence and transcript analysis; promoter activity analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgHMGR2 is a second HMGR isoform in P. ginseng. 2. Its expression increases with age, suggesting a role in age-dependent root ginsenoside accumulation. 3. Functional impact is weaker/less directly validated than PgHMGR1.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PgHMGR2 failed to complement Arabidopsis AtHMGR1 mutant, suggesting functional divergence despite similar catalytic activity.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts mevalonate diphosphate to isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP) by ATP-dependent decarboxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression does not significantly increase ginsenosides; instead promotes phytosterol accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in transgenic ginseng hairy roots; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgMVD is the last MVA enzyme for IPP production. 2. Its overexpression increases campesterol, stigmasterol and β-sitosterol with upregulated CAS. 3. It is not a strong driver of ginsenoside accumulation in the reported studies.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux is redirected toward phytosterols; β-amyrin decreased.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review explicitly contrasts its limited contribution to ginsenosides versus phytosterol biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses IPP and allylic prenyl diphosphates to form farnesyl diphosphate (FPP), precursor of squalene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases total ginsenoside content about 2.4-fold in transgenic ginseng hairy roots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in P. ginseng hairy roots and Centella asiatica; expression analysis of downstream genes; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgFPS provides FPP for triterpene and phytosterol synthesis. 2. Overexpression increases ginsenosides and upregulates downstream genes including PgCAS and PgDDS. 3. It is an effective engineering target upstream of squalene formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Also increases phytosterols and decreases β-amyrin in some transgenics.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts at a shared branch point feeding both ginsenoside and sterol pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: P. ginseng; tobacco; Eleutherococcus senticosus; yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Panax ginseng; Nicotiana tabacum; Eleutherococcus senticosus; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two FPP molecules to form squalene.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream committed entry to triterpene/sterol scaffold formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitous in ginseng tissues; enriched with PgSS3 in vascular bundle tissues and resin ducts of petioles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases total ginsenosides 1.6- to 3-fold and phytosterols about 2-fold in transgenic adventitious roots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in P. ginseng, tobacco and Eleutherococcus; yeast erg9 complementation; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgSS1 is a functional squalene synthase that channels FPP into squalene. 2. Overexpression upregulates downstream genes (SE, β-AS, CAS) and raises ginsenosides and phytosterols. 3. It is a major engineering target for boosting triterpene flux.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Squalene pool decreased due to rapid conversion downstream; also boosts phytosterols, not only ginsenosides.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: All three PgSS isoforms are biochemically functional; review emphasizes PgSS1 for strongest engineering evidence.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two FPP molecules to produce squalene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional SS isozyme likely contributing to triterpene/phytosterol biosynthesis in specific organs.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgSS2 is one of three functional ginseng SS genes. 2. Its expression is organ-restricted compared with ubiquitous PgSS1. 3. Yeast complementation confirms conserved SS enzymatic activity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific contribution to ginsenoside accumulation is less directly validated than PgSS1.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two FPP molecules to produce squalene.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in specific organs; preferentially expressed in vascular bundle tissues and resin ducts in petioles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional SS isozyme likely contributing to organ-specific triterpene biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sequence/transcript analysis; in situ hybridization; yeast erg9 complementation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgSS3 is a functional SS isozyme in P. ginseng. 2. It shows organ-specific expression, especially in vascular bundle/resin duct tissues. 3. It likely contributes to spatial control of ginsenoside/phytosterol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific engineering effect on ginsenosides not directly tested in the review.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of cyclization steps
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Abundant in all ginseng organs; preferentially expressed in vascular bundle tissue and resin ducts of petioles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Main SE isoform for ginsenoside biosynthesis; RNAi knockdown reduces ginsenosides.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi silencing in P. ginseng; expression analysis; methyl jasmonate treatment
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgSE1 catalyzes squalene epoxidation to 2,3-oxidosqualene, a key control step. 2. It is broadly expressed and induced by methyl jasmonate in roots. 3. Silencing lowers ginsenosides and causes compensatory increases in PgSE2/CAS and phytosterols.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: PgSE1 silencing enhances phytosterol accumulation via compensatory changes in PgSE2 and CAS.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review concludes PgSE1 mainly supports ginsenoside biosynthesis rather than phytosterol synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor of cyclization steps
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Weakly expressed except in petioles and flower buds; expressed in vascular bundle tissue and resin ducts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes more to phytosterol-associated branch than ginsenoside biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Sequence/transcript analysis; in situ hybridization; MJ response; indirect inference from PgSE1 RNAi compensation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgSE2 is a divergent SE isoform with restricted expression. 2. MJ suppresses PgSE2, unlike induction of PgSE1. 3. Compensatory upregulation in PgSE1-RNAi plants suggests distinct specialization, likely more related to phytosterol production.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review emphasizes functional divergence between PgSE1 and PgSE2.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: P. ginseng; tobacco; yeast; P. quinquefolius
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Panax ginseng; Nicotiana tabacum; Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Panax quinquefolius
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes 2,3-oxidosqualene to dammarenediol-II via dammarenyl cation intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: first committed step in ginsenoside synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential positive determinant of ginsenoside biosynthesis; RNAi reduces ginsenosides, overexpression enables dammarenediol-II production in heterologous systems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in erg7-deficient yeast; RNAi in P. ginseng; overexpression in tobacco; branch suppression via CAS-RNAi
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DDS catalyzes the first committed cyclization step toward dammarane-type ginsenosides. 2. DDS knockdown reduces ginsenoside production, whereas increased DDS activity raises ginsenosides. 3. Heterologous expression produces dammarenediol-II in yeast and tobacco, enabling synthetic biology production.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High in floral buds; induced by methyl jasmonate
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methyl jasmonate promotes expression; CAS suppression increases DDS activity
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Competition with CAS branch affects phytosterol supply; CAS suppression to favor DDS can slow hairy-root growth.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Contains six QW motifs and DCTAE motif; specialized OSC evolved in Panax.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates dammarenediol-II at C-12 to yield protopanaxadiol (PPD).
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms direct aglycone precursor of PPD-type ginsenosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of PPD and downstream PPD-type ginsenosides; silencing reduces growth and ginsenoside production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in yeast; silencing/RNAi in P. ginseng and P. quinquefolius homologs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP716A47/PPDS converts dammarenediol-II to PPD. 2. It is ubiquitously expressed and induced by methyl jasmonate in adventitious roots. 3. RNAi reduction lowers growth and ginsenoside production, supporting its essential role in PPD branch biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methyl jasmonate promotes PPDS expression in adventitious roots
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi lines showed reduced growth, indicating link between ginsenoside synthesis and plant development.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms direct aglycone precursor of PPT-type ginsenosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for PPT aglycone formation and downstream PPT-type ginsenosides.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPTS/CYP716A53v2 converts PPD to PPT. 2. It is ubiquitously expressed in ginseng organs. 3. It defines the PPT branch of dammarane-type ginsenoside biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not induced by methyl jasmonate according to cited work
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes MJ induces PPDS but not PPTS or OAS.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes β-amyrin at C-28 to generate oleanolic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Drives biosynthesis of oleanane-type saponins in P. ginseng.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP716A52v2/PgOAS converts β-amyrin to oleanolic acid. 2. It underlies biosynthesis of oleanane-type saponins such as ginsenoside Ro. 3. It is not induced by MJ in cited studies.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Relevant to minor oleanane-type ginsenoside branch rather than major dammarane branch.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to several dammarane intermediates, including dammarenediol, PPD, Rh2 and Rg3 at C-20 positions.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces multiple downstream glycosylated ginsenosides including compound K in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in yeast; combinatorial pathway reconstruction in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGTPg1 glycosylates multiple intermediates at C-20. 2. It enables production of compound K, F2 and Rd, and novel DMG in engineered yeast. 3. It suggests P. ginseng has latent capacity to synthesize compound K though it is not detectable in planta.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PgUGT71A27 is reported as identical with UGTPg1.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Rh2; F2; Rg3 (via downstream step); Rd (via downstream step)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to the C3 hydroxyl group of PPD and compound K, producing Rh2 and F2, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursors of more glycosylated ginsenosides
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Builds key glycosylated intermediates for production of Rg3 and Rd in engineered yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in yeast; combinatorial pathway engineering
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgUGT74AE2 catalyzes C3 glucosylation of PPD and compound K. 2. It forms Rh2 and F2 intermediates. 3. Together with PgUGT94Q2 it enables de novo Rg3 production in yeast.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to Rh2 and F2 to generate Rg3 and Rd, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly forms Rg3 and Rd from monoglucosylated intermediates in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Ectopic expression in yeast; combinatorial pathway engineering
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PgUGT94Q2 catalyzes an additional glucose transfer to Rh2 and F2. 2. It directly produces Rg3 and Rd. 3. It is a useful enzyme for synthetic biology production of specific ginsenosides.
